title: Results at a glance - Evaluation of the Public Health Agency of Canada’s Sexually Transmitted Blood Borne Infections activities 2018-19 to 2022-23 - Canada.ca
content:
## Results at a glance - Evaluation of the Public Health Agency of Canada’s Sexually Transmitted Blood Borne Infections activities 2018-19 to 2022-23
Sexually transmitted  and blood-borne infections (STBBI) such as HIV, hepatitis C,  and syphilis are a significant public health concern in Canada. In  2018, the federal government launched the Pan-Canadian Framework for Action on  STBBI, which sets out an integrated approach for Canada to achieve global STBBI  targets. The 2019 Government of Canada’s Five-Year Action Plan on STBBI  outlines priorities and a path to achieving the strategic goals under the  Framework, which are to:
- reduce  the incidence of STBBI in Canada
- improve  access to testing, treatment, and ongoing care and support
- reduce  stigma and discrimination that create vulnerabilities to STBBI
The Public Health  Agency of Canada (PHAC) coordinates the federal government’s public health  response to STBBI. The Infectious Diseases and Vaccines Program Branch leads  activities for the prevention and control of STBBI while the National  Microbiology Laboratory Branch leads laboratory and testing services. Both  branches support STBBI surveillance activities.
PHAC spent $252 million  on STBBI activities over five years (2018-19 to 2022-23),  including $85M in Salary and O&M, and $167M for Grants and  Contributions program.
This evaluation  examined the overall performance of PHAC's STBBI activities from 2018-19  to 2022-23.
- Performance data  analysis
- Financial data review

## What the evaluation found
PHAC, in collaboration  with FPT partners, advanced the goals of the Framework and Action Plan, despite  COVID-19-related delays. Notably, PHAC has helped Canada achieve two out of the  three HIV global testing and treatment targets for 2020 and one out of the  three 2025 targets. Further work is needed to address rising rates of bacterial  sexually transmitted infections, disproportionately higher rates of infection  in key populations, and continued stigma and discrimination that act as  barriers to testing, treatment, and ongoing care and support. Canada also has  significant progress to make to achieve 2030 targets for other STBBI.
PHAC was largely  successful in achieving the goals of its Contributions program, despite the  challenges faced during COVID-19 pandemic as PHAC and community-based partners  were able to pivot to virtual activities and expand testing access for  hard-to-reach communities.
Meaningful engagement  with people with lived and living experience of STBBI was a key factor in  achieving PHAC’s goals in this area as was the integrated approach of  addressing common risk factors and intervention opportunities. There is room  for improvement in ensuring timely collation, analysis, synthesis, and  dissemination of findings of recipients’ evaluation reports.
PHAC effectively used  partnerships and a variety of communication mechanisms to disseminate  information to target audiences including Canadians, health professionals, and  key populations. External partners recommend increasing community  consultations, particularly with people with lived and living experience from  key populations, to develop more relevant knowledge products.
PHAC uses  epidemiological data from surveillance and research to address the needs of key  populations and to inform decision making. There are opportunities to improve  linkages between the STBBI surveillance programs and other sections to enhance  the use of data to support decision making.
- Enhance       existing efforts, within PHAC’s mandate, to respond to rates of STBBI in       Canada.
- Identify       opportunities to build on existing action, within PHAC’s mandate, to       address STBBI-related stigma among key populations.
- Refine       performance evaluation of contributions recipients and collate, analyze,       and disseminate evaluation findings in a timely fashion.
- Refine       performance measurement approach for the renewed federal STBBI Action Plan       in collaboration with partners.
- Identify  opportunities to strengthen engagement of key populations across program  activities.
